narrator.personality: Senior security engineer. Calm but deadly serious. Keeps emphasizing HIPAA compliance and patient trust. Uses precise language.

story_beats.message: Compliance team just confirmed: the exposed files include patient intake forms with names, dates of birth, and insurance IDs. This is a HIPAA breach. We have 60 days to notify affected patients.
The security researcher who found it has 40,000 followers. She has not published yet but says she will give us 24 hours before public disclosure. Legal is drafting a breach notification.
CISO is on the bridge. She wants root cause, blast radius, and remediation in the next five minutes. We need to know exactly which files were accessed and by whom.
That does not explain the public access. The IAM roles are scoped correctly -- the issue is in the resource-based policy on the bucket itself. Look at who the bucket policy grants access to.

hints: The data is accessible without any AWS credentials. What kind of policy could allow unauthenticated access to an S3 bucket?
S3 has two types of access policies: IAM policies (identity-based) and bucket policies (resource-based). Have you checked the bucket policy?
Look at the Principal field in the bucket policy. Who is granted access?
Principal: * in an S3 bucket policy means anyone on the internet can perform the allowed actions. That is your root cause.

resolution.root_cause: S3 bucket policy on meridian-patient-documents grants s3:GetObject to Principal: * -- the bucket containing patient intake forms is publicly readable over the internet, exposing protected health information (PHI).

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the bucket policy allows public access via Principal: *
Player uses CloudTrail to determine when the bucket policy was modified and by whom
Player proposes restricting the Principal to specific IAM roles/accounts and enabling S3 Block Public Access
Player determines which files were accessed and the scope of the exposure using access logs

learning_objectives: Difference between S3 bucket policies (resource-based) and IAM policies (identity-based) for access control
Using CloudTrail to investigate who made API-level changes and when
S3 Block Public Access as an account-level and bucket-level guardrail against misconfigurations
Incident response workflow: detect, contain, investigate, remediate
